revisions_made: PRIMARY (gap 1): Added a full end-to-end serving-layer assessment as new section 6 covering src/polaris_v6 and the deployment boundary. It has: a topology subsection (6.0) documenting uvicorn --workers 1 / dramatiq --processes 1 --threads 2 / Caddy-only ingress / shared SQLite volume; an 8-row readiness table (6.1) with Present/Partial/Missing + file:line for each required item; a per-item evidence subsection (6.2); a prioritized serving-gaps list (6.3); and a serving-strengths balance subsection (6.4).

Serving-layer findings (all with file:line): S1 Admission control = Partial (1-concurrent-session BEGIN IMMEDIATE gate -> 409 at api/runs.py:91-119 + run_store.py:173-210, JWT auth on all routes, but no per-client rate limit); S2 Server-level request timeout = Missing (uvicorn started with no --timeout-keep-alive / no request deadline at v6_entrypoint.sh:54, no timeout middleware in app.py; the 30-min Dramatiq time_limit at actors.py:70 bounds the background JOB not the HTTP request); S3 Backpressure/concurrency = Partial (queue-based via 202+enqueue, single-session cap, but no uvicorn --limit-concurrency/--backlog and no Caddy connection cap); S4 Graceful shutdown = Partial (uvicorn+dramatiq defaults, but lifespan does not drain, and the StickyConnectionMiddleware.after_worker_shutdown hook is DEFINED but never registered -- get_broker() builds a plain RedisBroker despite the docstring claiming sticky connections); S5 Readiness = Partial (/health is static liveness at health.py:16-18 but used as the compose readiness gate; checks no dependency; no continuous /readyz); S6 SLOs/alerting = Missing (OTel is opt-in and trace-only via app.py:63-68, no metrics/Prometheus/alerts); S7 Multi-worker = Partial-mitigated-by-single-worker (WAL + busy_timeout=5000 + _INIT_LOCK + CAS transitions make API<->worker shared SQLite correct at 1 worker, but not validated for --workers N>1; single SQLite file over docker volume is a SQLITE_BUSY hazard; broker-init sentinel is per-process); S8 Auth = Present for single-tenant (fail-loud JWT substrate at auth.py:88-109).

SECONDARY (gap 2): Changed TIMEOUTS from Present to Partial in the checklist table (row 1 now Partial; row 1a rewritten). Added a 'Why Partial, not Present (revised)' rationale to section 3.1 (retitled 'Timeouts -- Partial') explaining that the zero-bare-calls grep is a SAMPLING technique that cannot follow dynamically-supplied timeout values -- evidenced by evidence_deepener.py:561,657,687 (timeout passed as a function parameter), searcher.py:2425-2428 (ClientTimeout threaded through), and graph_v2.py:745 (timeout=timeout_seconds variable).

Propagated the Partial timeout rating for consistency: rewrote the executive summary (line 13) to say timeouts are 'Partial rather than Present' with the reason, and softened the section-5 'genuinely strong' bullet with the same caveat. Added an executive-summary paragraph previewing the serving-layer assessment.
Updated the document scope line (line 4) from 'serving surface where it bounds the same runtime' to an explicit end-to-end assessment of src/polaris_v6 + deployment boundary in section 6.
Kept the entire existing pipeline-level checklist (sections 1-5) intact -- only softened two timeout claims for consistency; no pipeline evidence was removed.
residual_gaps: Two dynamic-timeout callsites cited as the reason for the Partial rating (evidence_deepener.py:561,657,687 and graph_v2.py:745) were confirmed to pass timeout via variable/parameter, but I did not exhaustively trace every one of the 102 files that reference 'timeout' to prove no branch yields timeout=None -- which is precisely the point of the Partial rating, so this residual is intentional/honest rather than a doc defect.

The serving-layer assessment is static/code-read only; I did not run the stack (docker compose up) to dynamically confirm, e.g., that a slow synchronous request actually hangs a worker thread (S2) or that SQLite contention manifests under load (S7). Claims are grounded in file:line code evidence, not runtime observation.

I discovered two additional unregistered Dramatiq middlewares -- StickyConnectionMiddleware (S4, now documented) and throttle.py (relevant to S3 backpressure, defined per broker.py docstring but not wired). I documented the sticky-connection one; the throttle middleware's non-registration is only implied via the S3 'no backpressure middleware' framing, not called out as its own line. A follow-up could add a dedicated note that broker.py's docstring (sticky + throttle mitigations) overstates what get_broker() actually wires.

The doc does not renumber sections 4/5 after inserting section 6 at the end (section 6 follows section 5). Section 4 (pipeline top gaps) and section 6.3 (serving top gaps) are now two separate prioritized lists; a reviewer wanting a single merged priority list across pipeline+serving would have to combine them manually.

revisions_made: PRIMARY (SSRF reachability/trust-boundary): Rewrote §2 with six new subsections (§2.1–§2.7) tracing all three input classes. Established via code that the served polaris_v6 API does NOT reach any vulnerable fetcher — the served /api/retrieval slice injects real_fetcher (app.py:119-127), which hits only fixed SERPER_ENDPOINT/S2_ENDPOINT (real_fetcher.py:42-43,101-118,139) and returns result URLs as strings without ever fetching their bodies; a grep of the entire served layer (src/polaris_v6/**, src/polaris_graph/api/**) for run_live_retrieval/_fetch_content_httpx_naive/live_retriever/domain_backends/access_bypass/utils.ingest returns zero hits. Showed the vulnerable fetchers are reached only by CLI/batch pipeline entrypoints (scripts/run_*honest*.py) and via LLM-harvested seed URLs (agentic_url_harvester.py:8).

Documented that upload (/upload reads file bytes only, upload.py:155), followup (answers over stored evidence contract, no HTTP client), and graph (reads AuditIR from disk) served routes also do not reach a fetcher.

Added explicit redirect-following analysis (follow_redirects=True at live_retriever.py:3015, domain_backends.py:211,246; allow_redirects=True at access_bypass.py:5590) and an explicit DNS-rebinding note stating a private-IP/host-string filter does NOT stop rebinding — requires resolve-once + IP pinning + per-hop revalidation, none of which exist.
Answered the exfiltration question: on the CLI/pipeline path fetched bodies flow into evidence/report (read-SSRF with response exfiltration, not blind); moot on the served API path per §2.2.

Answered the network-access question via a newly discovered compensating control: scripts/egress_lockdown.sh + config/egress_allowlist.txt install iptables/ip6tables DROP-default on 80/443 (egress_lockdown.sh:99-107), BUT explicitly ACCEPT 169.254.169.254/32 (egress_lockdown.sh:87-91), only cover ports 80/443, and are an operator-run deploy-time step with no code guarantee.

Re-rated SSRF conditionally (§2.7): HIGH as shipped; CRITICAL iff a deployment exposes a fetcher-reaching route to attacker-influenced input AND the runtime retains internal/metadata network access (egress lockdown absent or leaving 169.254 open). Condition stated verbatim.

Re-rated the POLARIS_AUTH_DISABLED=1 kill switch to HIGH (§1 new subsection + F-6): showed the same flag disables both the per-request auth dependency and verify_app_startup (auth.py:104-107), and that no production/env guard blocks it at startup (grep). Softened the 'fail-loud / production auth sound' characterization throughout (exec summary axis 1 → Med, reviewer takeaway, F-8 marked conditional).

Expanded the PII finding (§4 + F-3) beyond 'no redaction' to a compliance framing: added provider-retention accounting gap (OpenRouter as broker with unrecorded downstream provider/retention), no consent/lawful-basis capture, no DPA enforcement in code, sovereignty-brand mismatch; re-rated Medium→Medium–High with prioritized DPIA/zero-retention-routing/consent remediations (noting POLARIS_LLM_BACKEND=vllm already exists per egress_allowlist.txt:16-18).
Updated the executive summary table (§0), the consolidated findings table (§7, F-1/F-3/F-6/F-8 severities and evidence), and the reviewer priority list (§8) to reflect all re-ratings.
Verified all newly cited line numbers (real_fetcher.py:42-43, app.py:119-127, egress_lockdown.sh:87-91) by reading the files.
residual_gaps: The HIGH-vs-CRITICAL determination for SSRF is deployment-conditional and cannot be closed from code alone: a reviewer must confirm (a) no current/planned route mounts a run_live_retrieval/pipeline entrypoint behind the API, (b) egress_lockdown.sh is actually applied on every runtime host, and (c) whether batch-pipeline hosts have IMDS reachable / IMDSv2 hop-limit set. The doc states these as required deployment-validation items but they are unverifiable statically.

Whether POLARIS_AUTH_DISABLED=1 is present in any actual production deployment config (compose files, cloud-init, .env on the box) was not verified — those artifacts are outside the repo/runtime-code scope. The finding is rated on the code path permitting it, not on an observed production setting.
Provider (OpenRouter/Serper/S2) actual retention and training terms are external contractual facts not determinable from the repo; the DPIA recommendation depends on obtaining them.

The reachability negative for the served API is scoped to routes mounted in create_app() as read; a dynamically added router or a future wiring change (e.g. mounting a pipeline entrypoint) would invalidate it — noted in §2.7 as a validation item but not continuously enforced.

Did not exhaustively enumerate every transitive import of every served route to a fetcher; relied on a targeted grep of the served layer for the known fetcher symbols plus honest_pipeline/graph pipeline — a fetcher invoked through an unusual alias or dynamic import string would not be caught.

revisions_made: PRIMARY (codex gap 1): Added a new Section 6.5 'Row-by-row disposition of the authoritative worklist (ALL 210 RENAME rows)'. Read the entire NAME_RENAME_WORKLIST_validated.tsv (346 rows = 210 RENAME + 81 KEEP + 50 KEEP-NOTED + 4 KEEP-BUT-NOTED, verified with awk). Every RENAME row is classified into codex's four classes {SAFE-static, FILE-RENAME-fix-importers, NEEDS-ALIAS, DYNAMIC-HAZARD} with a one-line reason + file:line location. Built by mapping the worklist's own SAFETY column (105 SAFE + 12 TEXT-ONLY + 45 FILE-RENAME + 32 NEEDS-ALIAS + 16 DOMAIN-REVIEW = 210) onto the four classes, then hand-correcting. Presented as grouped tables/lists covering ALL rows with a reconciling roll-up table that accounts for all 210.

Split the 32 NEEDS-ALIAS rows into a NEEDS-ALIAS table (env-vars, run-id/manifest/mode_label/output-dir persisted strings) and a DYNAMIC-HAZARD table (content_integrity_junk persisted row-state dict key at junk_deletion_gate.py:110,244,270; beat_both_scorer.py and junk_deletion_gate.py string-imported/many-importer modules).

Verified importers in the current worktree for every src/ file rename and the public re-exported symbols (grep). Promoted ~5 worklist-'SAFE' public symbols to NEEDS-ALIAS with evidence: lethal_retrieve (re-exported retrieve/__init__.py:4,11 + 3 importers), run_honest_pipeline, run_v30_post_generation/merge_v30_into_manifest, EnhancedSourceScore, and the audit_ir HonestSweepJobRunner family.

ResearchStateV2 fix (codex gap 2): Rewrote §2.4, §3.2, §6-table, and §7. Added an evidence table proving ResearchStateV2 is used ONLY as intra-file type annotations in graph_v2.py; graph_v2 IS imported cross-file (scripts/live_server.py:561 imports build_and_run, NOT ResearchStateV2); graph_v2.py has NO __all__ (verified grep empty); no in-repo 'import *' exists. Concluded that 'checkpoint-serialization-safe' (fields persist, not class name) does NOT clear the import-name channel, and since a static grep cannot see out-of-repo importers, recommended a module-level compat alias (ResearchStateV2 = CragResearchState) rather than declaring SAFE. Same treatment applied to V3State (imported by graph_v3.py:25 + tests), V30SweepResult, and the package-re-exported HonestSweepJobRunner/Config/factory.

Documented the HonestSweepJobRunner string-dispatch registry: register_runner stores _RUNNERS[template_id] at job_runner.py:114-117 and inspector_router.py:418 registers under the string template_id='v30_clinical' (inspector_router.py:406,1452,1469-1476) — noted the class name is NOT the dispatch key (the string is), so class rename is dispatch-safe, but the factory-function name and __init__ re-export are real import-name contracts that must move together.

Removed ResearchStateV2 and HonestSweepJobRunner from the §7 'SAFE to rename' list; added them (plus V3State, V30SweepResult) to the §7 'REQUIRES AN ALIAS' list. Corrected the §6 'RENAME rows that are SAFE' paragraph which wrongly folded the public function is_row_content_junk and the persisted key content_integrity_junk into the SAFE bucket on a name-pattern basis.

Line-drift re-verification: flagged that worklist row 343 cites lethal.py:49 but the resolve('PG_LETHAL_SEED_K') literal is on lethal.py:50 in the current worktree (config_defaults.py:390 = '80' confirmed). Added an explicit 'LINE DRIFT' note in the NEEDS-ALIAS table.
Added cross-reference pointers (TL;DR and §6 header) directing readers to §6.5 as the authoritative full enumeration, with '§6.5 governs' where §6 and §6.5 differ.
residual_gaps: Cannot PROVE zero external (out-of-repo) importers for any renamed public symbol — a static grep only sees this repo. The doc now recommends compat aliases precisely because this is unprovable; if the reviewer has access to downstream consumer repos / the deployed API client, a grep there would let several 'NEEDS-ALIAS (recommended)' rows be downgraded to SAFE.

A handful of scripts/ 'SAFE' rows (e.g. strip_junk at pack_drb2.py:90) are public-ish functions inside scripts; I noted 'grep-confirm no cross-file caller before rename' rather than exhaustively verifying each of the ~105 SAFE rows' caller set individually — the private (_-prefixed) majority is safe by construction, but a per-row caller grep for the ~dozen non-underscore script functions was not run.

The pathB_capture.py importer count (30) is inflated by substring matches; I did not disambiguate exact import statements vs incidental 'pathB' string hits for that one module (it does have real importers, so its FILE-RENAME-fix-importers class is unaffected).

§6's original highlight tables were left largely intact (with corrections + a 'v6.5 governs' pointer) rather than deleted; a reviewer wanting a single non-redundant list will find §6 and §6.5 partially overlap by design.
